title: AI Governance Readiness Scorecard: The 4-Gate Score for Professional Services
subtitle: For operations leaders at mid-market accounting and legal firms. Score your firm against the 4 gates that separate the 6% cutting costs from the 94% collecting subscriptions.

persona_selector.text: Which best describes your firm's size today?

answers.label: Boutique (under 10 employees / fee earners)
Growing (11–30 employees)
Established mid-market (31–75 employees)
Scaling (76–150 employees, multi-office)

questions.text: How unified is the system where your client data actually lives?

answers.label: 1 — 6+ systems and nobody fully trusts any of them
2 — 3–5 systems with manual reconciliation
3 — 3 systems, reconciled weekly by someone
4 — Two systems that sync reliably
5 — One unified system with clean records

questions.text: How fast can you pull every active matter for one client across billing, documents, and email?

answers.label: 1 — Hours, and someone has to assemble it manually
2 — 30–60 minutes across several tools
3 — 10–30 minutes of digging across tools
4 — Under 10 minutes via a combined search
5 — Under 2 minutes via unified search

questions.text: How structured is the data your intake forms actually collect?

answers.label: 1 — Mostly free-text or paper
2 — A few dropdowns, mostly free-text
3 — Roughly half structured, half free-text
4 — 60–80% structured fields
5 — 80%+ structured with defined fields and dropdowns

questions.text: How recently have you audited duplicate or conflicting client records?

answers.label: 1 — Never, or we don't know how
2 — Once, years ago
3 — Within the last 2 years
4 — Within the last 12 months
5 — Within the last 6 months, on a recurring cadence

low.headline: Your data is the ceiling on every AI project you'll ever run
low.text: AI can't reason over 6 disconnected systems and free-text fields. Every pilot will stall here until the foundation is fixed. The first 90 days of payback live in data consolidation, not in buying tools.
steps: Map every system holding client data and rank each by trust level
Pick one system of record per data type (client, matter, billing) and stop dual-entry
Convert your top 3 intake forms to structured fields before touching any AI tool

mid.headline: The plumbing mostly works — the leaks are in reconciliation
mid.text: You have systems that sync, but manual reconciliation is still eating hours and introducing errors AI will amplify. The gap between 'mostly clean' and 'agent-ready' is closed by structured input data and scheduled dedup.
steps: Automate the reconciliation you're doing manually between your 2–3 core systems
Instrument a quarterly duplicate-record audit with a defined owner
Raise structured-field coverage on intake forms to 80%+

high.headline: Your foundation is agent-ready — now use it
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. With clean, unified data you can deploy narrow-scope AI agents that actually read your source of truth instead of guessing. Most firms your size don't get here; don't waste it.
steps: Layer a semantic search index on top of your unified client record
Deploy a matter-lookup assistant scoped to one practice group first
Run a quarterly data-drift check so foundation gains don't erode

questions.text: How much of your repeatable work is actually documented in a written SOP?

answers.label: 1 — Nothing is written down; it lives in people's heads
2 — A handful of tasks have notes in Google Docs
3 — Roughly half of core processes have SOPs
4 — Most core processes are documented and current
5 — 90%+ of repeatable work has versioned, current SOPs

questions.text: How clearly is the decision logic spelled out in your documented processes?

answers.label: 1 — Decisions are pure judgment, never written down
2 — SOPs say 'use your best judgment' at key steps
3 — Some decision rules exist but are inconsistent
4 — Most decisions have written criteria
5 — Every repeatable decision has if/then rules a new hire could follow

questions.text: How consistently are your SOPs updated when a process changes?

answers.label: 1 — Never; the doc and reality don't match
2 — Updated once in the last 2 years
3 — Updated when someone complains loudly enough
4 — Reviewed annually with a defined owner
5 — Quarterly review cycle with version history

questions.text: How independently can a new hire ramp on a core process using documentation alone?

answers.label: 1 — They can't; shadowing is the only way
2 — Docs get them 20% of the way
3 — Docs cover the basics, tribal knowledge covers the rest
4 — Docs get them 80% there with minimal shadowing
5 — Fully ramped from docs alone on defined workflows

low.headline: You can't automate what you can't describe
low.text: Documentable decision logic is a precondition for any AI system. If your processes live in partners' heads, you're not buying AI — you're buying confusion. Start by writing down the decisions, not the clicks.
steps: Pick your top 3 revenue-touching processes and map the decision points
Write if/then rules for the 5 most common judgment calls per process
Assign an owner for each SOP and lock in a quarterly review cadence

mid.headline: Docs exist — decision logic is the gap
mid.text: You've got SOPs, but AI stalls when the rules are fuzzy. The next unlock is converting 'use your best judgment' lines into explicit criteria. That's the difference between a doc and an agent-ready spec.
steps: Audit existing SOPs for every 'use your best judgment' phrase and replace with rules
Add a decision-log field to your top processes so edge cases get captured
Run a new-hire dry-run: can they execute from docs alone? Fix the gaps they hit

high.headline: Your ops are a spec — AI can read them
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. Firms with real process docs can point narrow-scope agents at them and get leverage fast. The trap now is letting docs drift while you build on top.
steps: Turn your tightest SOP into a prompted workflow an agent can execute
Instrument exception logging so doc drift surfaces inside weeks, not quarters
Pilot an internal assistant trained on your SOPs for junior staff Q&A

questions.text: How automated is your client intake from first contact to engagement letter?

answers.label: 1 — Fully manual; someone retypes everything
2 — Forms collect data, humans do everything else
3 — Some steps automated, handoffs still manual
4 — Mostly automated with human review at key gates
5 — Fully automated intake with human-in-the-loop sign-off

questions.text: How automated is your time capture, billing, and invoice cycle?

answers.label: 1 — Time entered manually, invoices assembled by hand
2 — Time tracked in a tool, billing still manual
3 — Invoices auto-generate but require heavy cleanup
4 — Mostly automated, partners review and send
5 — End-to-end automated with exception-only human review

questions.text: How automated is your document drafting and assembly for repeat matters?

answers.label: 1 — Every doc is copy-pasted from a prior file
2 — We use Word templates with fill-in blanks
3 — Templates with merge fields from one system
4 — Automated assembly from structured data on most repeat work
5 — Automated drafting with AI review on 80%+ of routine documents

low.headline: Manual work is the tax you're paying to avoid automation
low.text: At this level, every hour of growth equals an hour of headcount. That's the opposite of scale without scaling payroll. Pick the highest-volume workflow and automate its skeleton before adding any AI layer on top.
steps: Identify your top 3 highest-volume repeat workflows by hours consumed
Build the automation skeleton (intake → routing → output) before adding AI
Protect every automation with a human-in-the-loop checkpoint at the output stage

mid.headline: Automation exists — it just stops at the hard parts
mid.text: You've automated the easy steps. The 20% cost cut lives in the handoffs and judgment calls you're still doing manually. Close the gaps between steps before buying new tools.
steps: Map every manual handoff between existing automations and close them one by one
Add AI review to routine document drafts before partner sign-off
Instrument each workflow with a cycle-time metric so regressions are visible

high.headline: You're running a Hire a System operation
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. Fully automated workflows with human-in-the-loop gates are exactly the setup narrow-scope agents thrive in. Protect the leverage and push into the next tier of work.
steps: Push automation into the next category of work (complex matters, multi-party)
Benchmark your cycle times against firms 2x your size and find the deltas
Layer analytics on workflow exceptions to surface the next automation target

questions.text: How consistently do automated outputs pass through a defined human review gate?

answers.label: 1 — No review; outputs ship directly to clients
2 — Review happens sometimes, depends on the person
3 — Review exists but is inconsistent across teams
4 — Every automated output has a named reviewer
5 — Defined review gates with logged sign-off on every output

questions.text: How clearly defined is the escalation path when an automated workflow hits an edge case?

answers.label: 1 — Things just break and nobody knows who owns it
2 — Someone eventually figures it out informally
3 — Escalation rules exist but aren't always followed
4 — Named owners and defined escalation paths
5 — Logged exceptions, named owners, and SLA on resolution

questions.text: How clearly does your firm define what AI tools can and cannot touch?

answers.label: 1 — No policy; staff use whatever they want
2 — Verbal guidance only; nothing written
3 — A basic policy exists but is rarely referenced
4 — Written policy with defined approved tools and data rules
5 — Policy plus training, audit log, and quarterly review

questions.text: How reliably can you reconstruct who approved what on an automated output 6 months later?

answers.label: 1 — We can't; there's no log
2 — Maybe through email, with effort
3 — Partial logs across disconnected tools
4 — Centralized logs with searchable history
5 — Full audit trail with immutable approval records

low.headline: You have no governance — just tools and hope
low.text: Without human-in-the-loop design, AI in a professional services firm is a malpractice risk, not an asset. Before any further automation, stand up the review gates and the policy. This is non-negotiable.
steps: Write a one-page AI usage policy naming approved tools and data rules
Assign a named reviewer for every automated output touching a client
Stand up a simple approval log that captures who signed off on what

mid.headline: Controls exist on paper — consistency is the gap
mid.text: You've got the outlines of governance, but enforcement is uneven across teams. That's where risk hides. Tighten the review loops and make the audit trail the default, not the exception.
steps: Move approval sign-offs into a single system with logged history
Define SLAs on exception resolution and assign owners per workflow
Run a quarterly review of AI usage against your written policy

high.headline: Your governance is a moat
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. Mid-market firms with real AI controls can say yes to work larger competitors can't safely touch. Make it visible in pitches and procurement responses.
steps: Package your AI governance posture as a pitch asset for enterprise clients
Add anomaly detection on approval patterns to catch drift early
Extend the audit trail to cover vendor AI usage across your stack

questions.text: How rigorously do you track the ROI of AI and automation investments?

answers.label: 1 — We don't; we just feel like it's helping
2 — We track spend but not return
3 — Some workflows have before/after metrics
4 — Every investment has a defined ROI target and review
5 — Quarterly ROI review with kill criteria for under-performers

questions.text: How disciplined is your firm about expected payback window on new automation?

answers.label: 1 — We buy tools hoping they'll pay off someday
2 — We expect payback within a year or two
3 — We target 6–12 month payback loosely
4 — We require payback inside 6 months
5 — We enforce a 90-day payback rule before scaling

questions.text: How consistently do you audit SaaS and AI subscriptions against actual usage?

answers.label: 1 — Never; we don't even know what's active
2 — Rarely, when finance complains
3 — Annual review, sometimes skipped
4 — Quarterly review with usage data
5 — Monthly review with auto-flagging of underused tools

low.headline: You're buying tools, not outcomes
low.text: 94% of firms pilot AI. Only the ones who enforce payback discipline hit the 20% cost cut. Without ROI rails, you'll keep stacking subscriptions and wondering why margins aren't moving.
steps: Attach a dollar-value target to every automation project before it starts
Enforce the 90-Day Payback Rule on new tools: no payback, no renewal
Run a one-time audit of every active SaaS tool and kill the unused ones

mid.headline: You track spend — now track the return
mid.text: You've got the basics of financial hygiene, but payback discipline is inconsistent. Firms that scale without scaling payroll treat every tool like a hire: quarterly performance review, clear kill criteria.
steps: Define ROI targets per workflow and review them every quarter
Add kill criteria so under-performers get cut, not renewed by default
Tie automation spend to headcount avoidance in your P&L reporting

high.headline: You're running AI like an operating discipline, not a trend
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. Firms at this level have the governance and the economics to compound. The next lever is portfolio-level ROI: treating automations like a book of assets.
steps: Build a portfolio view of all automations with ROI trends over time
Reallocate budget quarterly from low-return to high-return workflows
Benchmark your cost-to-serve per matter against top-quartile peers

ctas.headline: Want a working call on your 4-Gate score?
ctas.description: Book a 30-minute working call where I'll walk you through your exact answers, stack them against accounting and legal firms your size, and pinpoint the ONE fix I'd prioritize first. No pitch — if we both see signal, we keep going.

ctas.headline: Under 10 people? You're early for a custom audit.
ctas.description: Automation ROI at your scale is real but payback is longer and the governance overhead is harder to justify. Follow me on LinkedIn — I post tactical accounting and legal automation breakdowns 3x/week. When you cross 10 fee earners, come back and retake; the CTA will change.

ctas.headline: You're running tight — here's what mid-market firms your size miss next.
ctas.description: At your score, the obvious leaks are closed. The next gains come from portfolio-level ROI discipline, narrow-scope agents pointed at your SOPs, and packaging your governance posture as a procurement asset. I'm writing teardowns on each — follow along.

ctas.headline: Get the weekly professional services ops teardown
ctas.description: One tactical case study plus one n8n workflow breakdown per week. How mid-market accounting and legal firms run lean and hit the 20% cost cut. No fluff, no pitch.
